title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Childhood Emotional Neglect by Respect Culture
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy can heal childhood emotional neglect by addressing bodily responses and nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding Childhood Emotional Neglect (CEN)

Childhood Emotional Neglect (CEN) is a pervasive but often overlooked form of trauma where a child's emotional needs are consistently unmet by caregivers. Unlike physical abuse or overt neglect, CEN is characterized by the absence of emotional validation, empathy, and attunement. As Dr. Jonice Webb, a leading researcher on CEN, explains, children growing up with emotional neglect often learn to suppress their feelings and disconnect from their internal experiences.1 This disconnection can manifest as chronic feelings of emptiness, low self-worth, and difficulty identifying or expressing emotions.

Why Traditional Talk Therapy Often Falls Short

Many survivors of CEN report feeling stuck in traditional talk therapy. This is because the root of their suffering lies not only in cognitive patterns but deeply embedded in their bodies and nervous systems. As Bessel van der Kolk, MD, describes in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma is stored in the body and nervous system, not just the brain.2 For CEN survivors, the internal signals of emotional need were ignored or invalidated, leading to an ingrained bodily habit of emotional shutdown. This makes purely verbal approaches insufficient without addressing the somatic experience.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is an umbrella term for body-centered approaches that focus on the interplay between mind and body in healing trauma. It recognizes that trauma and neglect alter the nervous system’s regulation and bodily sensations. According to Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, trauma disrupts the natural rhythms of the nervous system, leaving individuals stuck in states of hyperarousal or dissociation.3 Somatic therapy uses techniques such as breathwork, body awareness, movement, and touch to help individuals access and discharge these stuck states, restoring nervous system balance.

The Nervous System and Emotional Neglect: Insights from Polyvagal Theory

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a critical framework for understanding the impact of CEN on the autonomic nervous system.4 When emotional needs are consistently unmet, the nervous system adapts by shifting into protective states. This can mean chronic activation of the dorsal vagal complex, responsible for shutdown and dissociation, or heightened sympathetic arousal, causing anxiety and hypervigilance. Survivors of CEN often live in these dysregulated states without the tools to self-soothe or reconnect with their emotions.

“Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you.” – Gabor Maté

How Somatic Therapy Supports Healing from CEN

Somatic therapy offers pathways to re-establish connection with the body’s sensations and the nervous system’s natural rhythms. Through gentle, guided awareness practices, CEN survivors learn to notice where emotions live in the body, identify sensations linked to emotional experiences, and practice self-regulation techniques. Deb Dana, a leading clinician in applying Polyvagal Theory, emphasizes that this process helps rebuild the internal sense of safety and connection necessary for emotional healing.5

  • Body Awareness: Tracking bodily sensations helps survivors recognize emotions early, counteracting the habitual emotional numbness.
  • Nervous System Regulation: Techniques like paced breathing and grounding activate the parasympathetic nervous system to calm hyperarousal.
  • Resourcing: Identifying internal and external resources creates a felt sense of safety and empowerment.
  • Movement and Touch: Somatic therapies may incorporate mindful movement or therapeutic touch to release held tension and trauma.

Respect Culture’s Approach to Somatic Therapy for CEN

Respect Culture integrates somatic healing with a trauma-informed, culturally sensitive framework. Recognizing that emotional neglect often intersects with systemic issues like racism, poverty, and intergenerational trauma, Respect Culture honors the whole person’s identity and experience. Their approach involves:

  • Creating a safe, non-judgmental therapeutic container.
  • Combining somatic techniques with narrative and relational healing.
  • Empowering survivors to develop self-compassion and reclaim emotional autonomy.
  • Utilizing community and cultural resources to support holistic healing.

Research Evidence Supporting Somatic Therapy in CEN

While research specifically on somatic therapy for CEN is emerging, studies on trauma and somatic interventions provide strong support. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy has demonstrated efficacy in reducing trauma symptoms by combining somatic and cognitive work.6 Francine Shapiro’s Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR), which incorporates body awareness, also shows promise in addressing trauma-related dissociation common in CEN.7 Overall, somatic approaches have been linked to improved emotional regulation, reduced anxiety, and increased embodiment in trauma survivors.

Practical Steps for Self-Directed Somatic Healing

If you’re a survivor of CEN looking to begin somatic healing on your own, here are some foundational steps based on expert recommendations:

  • Develop Body Awareness: Spend daily moments checking in with your body. Notice where you feel tension, numbness, or other sensations.
  • Practice Nervous System Regulation: Use simple breathwork, such as slow diaphragmatic breathing, to calm the nervous system.
  • Identify Safe Spaces and People: Build your internal and external resources by recalling places or people that evoke safety.
  • Journal about Sensations and Emotions: Track your internal experiences beyond thoughts to include bodily feelings.
  • Engage in Gentle Movement: Activities like yoga, walking, or stretching can increase body connection.

Remember, somatic healing is a gradual process. If feelings become overwhelming, seek support from a trained somatic therapist.

Conclusion: Reclaiming Connection Through Somatic Healing

Childhood Emotional Neglect leaves invisible wounds that traditional talk therapy alone cannot fully address. By tuning into the body and nervous system, somatic therapy offers survivors a powerful path to reconnect with their emotions, restore self-trust, and build resilience. Respect Culture’s trauma-informed, culturally attuned approach ensures that healing is holistic and honors each survivor’s unique story. As Gabor Maté so eloquently puts it, healing is about reclaiming what was lost inside. Somatic therapy is one of the most direct ways to do just that.

References

  1. Webb, J. (2013). Running on Empty: Overcome Your Childhood Emotional Neglect. New Harbinger Publications.
  2. van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score. Viking.
  3. Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  4. Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  5. Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  6. Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  7. Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  8. Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No: Exploring the Stress-Disease Connection. Wiley.

faqs.a: Childhood emotional neglect occurs when a child's emotional needs are consistently ignored or invalidated by caregivers, leading to disconnection from feelings.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy help with emotional neglect?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy helps by reconnecting survivors with their bodily sensations and regulating the nervous system, addressing trauma stored in the body.

faqs.q: Can I practice somatic healing on my own?
faqs.a: Yes, basic practices like body awareness, breathwork, and gentle movement can be done independently, but professional guidance is recommended for deep trauma.

faqs.q: Is somatic therapy supported by research?
faqs.a: Yes, multiple researchers like Peter Levine and Pat Ogden have shown somatic approaches effectively reduce trauma symptoms and improve emotional regulation.
